cards.q: Define seizure, seizure disorder, and epilepsy, and explain how they differ.
cards.lecturer: A seizure is a discrete clinical event (behavioural, emotional, motor or sensory) that reflects a temporary physiologic dysfunction of the brain, characterized by excessive and hypersynchronous discharge of cortical neurons. Seizure disorder is a general term used to include any of several disorders, including epilepsy, febrile seizures, and possibly single seizures and symptomatic seizures secondary to metabolic, infectious or other aetiologies. Epilepsy is a chronic disorder or group of disorders characterized by seizures that usually recur unpredictably in the absence of a consistent provoking factor.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: These three words are NOT interchangeable

Students use them loosely. Examiners test whether you know the difference precisely.

>> **SEIZURE = one event.**
>> **EPILEPSY = a tendency to recurrent, unprovoked seizures.**
>> **SEIZURE DISORDER = the umbrella term covering both.**

---

## Step 2: Dissect the definition of SEIZURE

>> "A **discrete clinical event**... characterized by **excessive and hypersynchronous discharge of cortical neurons**."

---

**"Discrete"** = it has a clear start and end. It is an **event**, not a state.

**"Excessive and hypersynchronous"** — this is the key phrase.

Normally, cortical neurons fire **independently**, at different times — like a room full of people talking.

>> In a seizure they fire **all together, in synchrony** — like the whole room suddenly **chanting in unison**.

**Hypersynchronous = abnormally synchronized.** That is the electrical essence of a seizure.

---

## Step 3: Notice the FOUR types of clinical event

The definition says behavioural, emotional, **motor** OR **sensory**.

>> A seizure is **not always convulsions.** It can be a strange smell, a feeling of fear, a blank stare — depending on which part of the cortex is discharging.

---

## Step 4: Now EPILEPSY — find the two key words

>> "Seizures that usually **RECUR** unpredictably **in the absence of a consistent provoking factor**."

---

**Two conditions must both be true:**

- **RECUR** — more than one
- **No provoking factor** — **unprovoked**

---

>> **Epilepsy = RECURRENT + UNPROVOKED seizures.**

A child who has one seizure does not have epilepsy. A child who fits only with fever does not have epilepsy (those are **provoked**).

---

## Step 5: SEIZURE DISORDER — the umbrella

It **includes** epilepsy, febrile seizures, single seizures, and symptomatic seizures.

>> So epilepsy is a **subset** of seizure disorders — not a synonym.

---

## Step 6: Why the distinction matters clinically

**Labelling a child "epileptic" has huge consequences** — years of medication, stigma, driving and career implications.

>> You must **not** call a child epileptic after a single seizure, or after purely provoked (e.g. febrile) seizures.

**Getting these definitions right protects the child from a wrong lifelong label.**

---

## Putting it all together

A **seizure** is a **single discrete event** of **hypersynchronous** cortical discharge — the neurons that should fire independently all fire together.

**Epilepsy** is **recurrent, unprovoked** seizures — both words required.

**Seizure disorder** is the **umbrella** covering epilepsy, febrile seizures and the rest.

The practical point: **never call a child epileptic after one seizure or provoked seizures** — the label is lifelong.
cards.tie: These definitions are the foundation of the whole topic and are asked directly, but they also carry real weight in practice. Diagnosing epilepsy commits a child to prolonged treatment and social consequences, so knowing that it requires recurrent AND unprovoked seizures protects children from being wrongly labelled after a single or provoked event.

cards.q: Define febrile seizure precisely, giving every clause your lecturer includes.
cards.lecturer: Febrile seizures are seizures that occur between the ages of 6 and 60 months (peak 12-18 months) with a temperature of 38 degrees C (100.4 F) or higher, that are not the result of CNS infection or any metabolic imbalance, and that occur in the absence of a history of prior afebrile seizures.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: This definition is a checklist — EVERY clause matters

Examiners award marks clause by clause, and each clause **excludes something dangerous.**

---

## Step 2: Break it into five conditions

>> **1. Age 6–60 months** (peak 12–18 months)
>> **2. Temperature ≥ 38°C (100.4°F)**
>> **3. NOT due to CNS infection**
>> **4. NOT due to metabolic imbalance**
>> **5. NO prior afebrile seizure**

---

## Step 3: Understand WHY each clause is there

---

**Age 6–60 months — why this window?**

The immature brain of this age is **uniquely susceptible** to fever-provoked seizures.

>> A "febrile seizure" **outside this age range** should make you doubt the diagnosis. A fitting 4-month-old or 7-year-old needs a **different explanation** — think infection or epilepsy.

---

**Temperature ≥ 38°C — the fever must be real.**

No fever → not a febrile seizure by definition.

---

**NOT CNS infection — this is the crucial exclusion.**

>> **Meningitis and encephalitis also cause fever AND seizures.**

If the seizure is due to the infection **reaching the brain**, it is **NOT** a febrile seizure — it is a **seizure caused by meningitis**, which is an emergency.

**This clause is why you must rule out meningitis before ever diagnosing a febrile seizure.**

---

**NOT metabolic — exclude the treatable mimics.**

>> Low **glucose, sodium, calcium** can all cause seizures. A febrile child who is also hypoglycaemic is having a **metabolic** seizure, not a febrile one.

---

**NO prior afebrile seizure — this separates it from epilepsy.**

>> If the child has **already** had seizures **without** fever, they have **epilepsy**, and this is just an epileptic seizure triggered by fever — a different diagnosis with different implications.

---

## Step 4: See the pattern

>> **Every "NOT" clause excludes a more serious diagnosis:** meningitis, metabolic derangement, epilepsy.

The definition is really saying: *"a benign, fever-triggered seizure in a susceptible young child — once you have ruled out everything dangerous."*

---

## Step 5: The practical consequence

Because the definition **requires excluding CNS infection**, your workup **must** actively look for meningitis —

>> especially an **LP in a child under 18 months**, where meningitis may not show neck stiffness.

---

## Putting it all together

Febrile seizure = **age 6–60 months + fever ≥ 38°C + NO CNS infection + NO metabolic cause + NO prior afebrile seizure.**

The three **"NOT"** clauses each **exclude a dangerous mimic** — meningitis, metabolic derangement, and epilepsy.

That is why you **cannot diagnose a febrile seizure until you have excluded meningitis**, and why the age window itself is a diagnostic clue.
cards.tie: Febrile seizures are the single commonest seizure presentation you will meet in paediatric practice, and this exact definition is examined repeatedly. Each clause is a safety check — getting the definition right is what stops you from mislabelling meningitis, hypoglycaemia or epilepsy as a benign febrile seizure.

cards.q: Distinguish provoked from unprovoked seizures, and genetic from structural epilepsy.
cards.lecturer: Provoked seizures occur secondary to an acute problem. Most seizures in children are provoked by somatic disorders originating outside the brain such as high fever, infection etc. With the elimination of the stimulus, seizure stops. Unprovoked seizure has no obvious stimulus - a brain disorder producing recurrent spontaneous paroxysmal discharges of cerebral neurons. Genetic epilepsy (previously idiopathic) are seizures that occur without an identifiable cause in a patient with entirely normal findings on neurologic examination and of normal intelligence. Structural epilepsy (previously symptomatic) refers to an epilepsy syndrome caused by an underlying structural brain disorder that may or may not be genetic.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Provoked vs unprovoked — the deciding question

>> **Is there a TRIGGER?**

- **Yes, and removing it stops the seizures** → **PROVOKED**
- **No trigger, the brain does it spontaneously** → **UNPROVOKED**

---

## Step 2: Understand provoked seizures

>> "Secondary to an **acute problem**... with elimination of the stimulus, **the seizure stops**."

The seizure is a **reaction** to something — usually **outside the brain** (fever, infection, low sugar).

---

**The key insight:** the brain itself may be **completely normal**. It is responding to an abnormal environment.

>> Fix the environment (bring down the fever, correct the glucose) and the seizures **stop for good.**

**Most childhood seizures are provoked** — which is exactly why most children who seize do **not** have epilepsy.

---

## Step 3: Understand unprovoked seizures

>> "**No obvious stimulus** — a brain disorder producing **recurrent spontaneous** discharges."

Here the problem is **in the brain itself**. Nothing external is setting it off.

---

>> **Recurrent + spontaneous + unprovoked = the definition of EPILEPSY.**

This links directly to the epilepsy definition: recurrent unprovoked seizures.

---

## Step 4: Now genetic vs structural — learn the OLD names too

The terminology was updated, but examiners use both:

| New term | Old term |
|---|---|
| **Genetic** | **Idiopathic** |
| **Structural** | **Symptomatic** |

---

## Step 5: Genetic (idiopathic) epilepsy — the "everything normal" epilepsy

>> "Without an identifiable cause... **entirely normal neurologic examination** and **normal intelligence**."

---

**Two normals to remember:**

- **Normal examination**
- **Normal IQ**

>> The child looks and functions **completely normally** — the only problem is the seizures. There is a **genetic predisposition** but no visible brain lesion.

---

## Step 6: Structural (symptomatic) epilepsy — something IS wrong

>> "Caused by an **underlying STRUCTURAL brain disorder**."

There is a **physical abnormality** — a malformation, scar, tumour, or damage from asphyxia or infection.

---

>> **Genetic = normal brain, normal child. Structural = a physical brain lesion.**

The neuroimaging is normal in genetic epilepsy and **abnormal** in structural epilepsy.

---

## Step 7: Why these distinctions matter

- **Provoked vs unprovoked** decides whether the child has **epilepsy** at all
- **Genetic vs structural** predicts **prognosis** — genetic epilepsies often have normal development and better outcomes, while structural epilepsies carry the burden of the underlying lesion

---

## Putting it all together

**Provoked** seizures have a **trigger** and stop when it is removed — the brain may be normal. **Unprovoked** seizures are **spontaneous**, and recurrent unprovoked seizures **are epilepsy**.

**Genetic (idiopathic)** epilepsy = **normal examination, normal intelligence, no lesion.** **Structural (symptomatic)** epilepsy = **an underlying structural brain disorder.**

Together these tell you **whether it is epilepsy** and **how the child is likely to do.**
cards.tie: These distinctions determine both diagnosis and prognosis. Recognising a provoked seizure prevents an unnecessary epilepsy label, and distinguishing genetic from structural epilepsy shapes your investigation (whether imaging is needed) and your counselling of the family about the likely outcome.

cards.q: Describe the 2017 ILAE classification of seizures — the four onset categories, and how focal and generalized seizures differ.
cards.lecturer: The 2017 ILAE operational classification divides epileptic seizures into four categories based on the presumed mode of seizure onset: focal-onset, generalized-onset, unknown onset, and unclassified. Generalized seizures: the clinical and EEG changes indicate synchronous involvement of both hemispheres. Focal seizures: activation of a system of neurons limited to part of one cerebral hemisphere. Unknown onset: if there is not enough clinical information to determine if the seizure is focal or generalized. Unclassified: if the clinical characteristics are unusual and a determination of onset cannot be made despite an adequate workup. Focal seizures are further described by awareness: aware, or impaired awareness. Focal to bilateral tonic-clonic was previously called secondary generalized seizure.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: The classification asks ONE question first

>> **Where did the seizure START?**

Everything flows from onset. That is why it is called an "onset" classification.

---

## Step 2: The four categories

>> **1. FOCAL-onset** — starts in **one part of one hemisphere**
>> **2. GENERALIZED-onset** — starts in **both hemispheres at once**
>> **3. UNKNOWN onset** — not enough information to tell
>> **4. UNCLASSIFIED** — odd features, cannot decide even after full workup

---

## Step 3: Focal vs generalized — the precise definitions

**FOCAL:**

>> "Activation of a system of neurons **limited to part of ONE cerebral hemisphere**."

One spot. One side. A **local** electrical storm.

---

**GENERALIZED:**

>> "Clinical and EEG changes indicate **SYNCHRONOUS involvement of BOTH hemispheres**."

The whole brain, both sides, all at once.

---

>> **Focal = one corner of one side. Generalized = the whole brain simultaneously.**

---

## Step 4: The difference between "unknown" and "unclassified"

Students confuse these. The distinction is about **why** you cannot classify it.

>> **UNKNOWN onset** — you **lack information** (no witness, nobody saw the start)
>>
>> **UNCLASSIFIED** — you have the information, but the features are **so unusual** you still cannot decide, even after adequate workup

---

**Unknown = not enough data. Unclassified = data present but bizarre.**

---

## Step 5: For focal seizures, add the AWARENESS descriptor

Once you know a seizure is focal, ask a **second** question:

>> **Was awareness KEPT or IMPAIRED?**

- **Focal AWARE** = old **"simple partial"**
- **Focal with IMPAIRED AWARENESS** = old **"complex partial"**

---

>> This is the old-to-new translation examiners want: **simple = aware, complex = awareness impaired.**

---

## Step 6: The third focal descriptor

>> **Focal to bilateral tonic-clonic** = old **"secondary generalized seizure."**

A seizure that **starts focal** and then **spreads** to become a generalized tonic-clonic convulsion.

---

**The clue at the bedside:** an **aura** before the convulsion means it started **focally** — because the aura IS the focal onset before it spread.

---

## Step 7: Why onset drives everything

- **Focal** onset points to a **structural/local** cause → needs imaging
- **Generalized** onset points to a more **diffuse/genetic** process
- The **drug choice** differs between focal and generalized seizures

---

>> **Getting the onset right is the first step to the right cause and the right drug.**

---

## Putting it all together

The 2017 classification sorts seizures by **onset**: **focal** (one part of one hemisphere), **generalized** (both hemispheres synchronously), **unknown** (insufficient data), and **unclassified** (unusual despite workup).

**Focal** seizures get a second label — **aware** (old simple) or **impaired awareness** (old complex) — and a focal seizure that spreads is **focal to bilateral tonic-clonic** (old secondary generalized).

**Onset is the master question** because it points to the cause and guides the drug.
cards.tie: The 2017 classification is the current framework and examiners expect you to use its terminology while recognising the old terms. Classifying by onset is not academic — it determines whether you image the brain looking for a focal lesion and which anti-seizure medication is appropriate, since focal and generalized seizures respond to different drugs.

cards.q: Explain the pathophysiology of seizures — the role of glutamate and GABA, and the three mechanisms by which seizures arise.
cards.lecturer: Physiology of nerve impulse propagation: excitation propagation is by glutamate and aspartate; inhibition propagation is by GABA (gamma-aminobutyric acid). Both excitatory and inhibitory control mechanisms act to allow appropriate firing and prevent inappropriate excitation of the cell. Seizures occur due to: excitation of the neuron by excessive, frequent or sustained activation of glutamate receptors, increasing the excitability of the brain; inhibition failure due to disruption in GABA production and its receptors; or a combination of both influences. There are four distinct, often sequential, mechanistic processes: (1) underlying aetiology, (2) epileptogenesis, (3) epileptic state of increased excitability, and (4) seizure-related neuronal injury.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: The whole thing is a SEE-SAW

The brain is balanced between two opposing forces:

>> **GLUTAMATE (and aspartate) = EXCITATION = the accelerator**
>>
>> **GABA = INHIBITION = the brake**

---

**In health, they balance** — the lecturer's words: they "allow appropriate firing and **prevent inappropriate excitation**."

The brake normally stops neurons firing when they shouldn't.

---

## Step 2: A seizure = the see-saw tips toward excitation

There are **three ways** to tip it, and you should be able to name all three:

---

**Way 1 — TOO MUCH ACCELERATOR**

>> "Excessive, frequent or sustained activation of **glutamate** receptors."

The excitatory system is **over-active**.

---

**Way 2 — FAILED BRAKE**

>> "Disruption in **GABA** production and its receptors."

The inhibitory system **fails** — nothing stops the over-firing.

---

**Way 3 — BOTH**

Too much accelerator **and** a failed brake together.

---

>> **Seizure = too much glutamate, OR too little GABA, OR both.**

---

## Step 3: Why this is the most USEFUL slide in the lecture

Because **the drugs make sense only if you know this see-saw.**

Every anti-seizure drug works by pushing the see-saw back toward inhibition:

- **Cut the accelerator** → block Na⁺ channels → **phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate**
- **Boost the brake** → enhance GABA → **phenobarbitone, benzodiazepines**

---

>> **Learn the see-saw once, and the entire drug table becomes logical instead of memorised.**

---

## Step 4: The four sequential processes — the life story of epilepsy

The lecturer gives four stages, in order:

>> **1. Underlying aetiology** — the initial insult (trauma, infection, malformation)
>>
>> **2. Epileptogenesis** — the brain gradually **rewires** into a seizure-prone state
>>
>> **3. Epileptic state of increased excitability** — the brain is now **primed** to seize
>>
>> **4. Seizure-related neuronal injury** — each seizure **damages** neurons further

---

## Step 5: The vicious cycle hidden in stage 4

Look at stage 4: seizures **injure neurons**.

>> And injured neurons are **more likely to seize** — feeding back into more excitability.

**"Seizures beget seizures."** This is the rationale for treating status epilepticus quickly — every minute of seizing causes more injury, which causes more seizing.

---

## Step 6: Why is there a delay (epileptogenesis)?

Note stage 2 — after a brain insult, epilepsy often does **not** appear immediately.

>> There is a **latent period** while the brain reorganizes into an epileptic network. This is why a child can develop epilepsy **months or years** after a head injury or meningitis.

---

## Putting it all together

Seizures are a **see-saw** between **glutamate (excitation/accelerator)** and **GABA (inhibition/brake)**.

A seizure happens three ways: **too much glutamate, too little GABA, or both.**

This directly explains the drugs — they either **cut the accelerator (block Na⁺ channels)** or **boost the brake (enhance GABA)**.

And epilepsy evolves through **four stages** — aetiology, epileptogenesis, an excitable state, and neuronal injury — with the last feeding back so that **seizures beget seizures.**
cards.tie: The glutamate-GABA balance is the single concept that makes the entire drug table comprehensible rather than something to be memorised, so it is worth real effort. Understanding that seizures cause neuronal injury which promotes further seizures is also the rationale for treating status epilepticus urgently rather than waiting.

cards.q: Describe the generalized tonic-clonic seizure and its EEG, and contrast the two focal seizure types.
cards.lecturer: Generalized tonic-clonic seizure (GTCS) is the commonest clinical manifestation: loss of tone and consciousness, generalized tonic followed by rhythmic clonic contraction alternating with relaxation of all muscle groups, with autonomic effects such as incontinence, apnoea and cyanosis. EEG (ictal): burst of spikes and polyspikes at 10-15 Hz in the tonic phase, and diffuse high voltage slow waves in the clonic phase. Focal onset awareness intact (formerly simple partial): mostly motor activity, versive seizures with head turning and conjugate eye movement indicating a lesion in the opposite frontal lobe, plus or minus aura, duration 10-20 sec, no automatism, no postictal phase, consciousness intact. Focal to bilateral (temporal lobe, formerly complex partial): aura, impaired consciousness, automatic behaviour in 50-70%, duration 1-2 minutes, a postictal phase, may result in secondary generalization.
cards.imgCaption: The tonic and clonic phases (left) and the corresponding EEG changes (right)
cards.explain:
## Step 1: GTCS — decode the name

>> **Tonic** = **sustained** stiffening (the body goes rigid, arches)
>> **Clonic** = **rhythmic jerking** (contract–relax–contract–relax)

So a **tonic-clonic** seizure is **stiffen THEN jerk**, in that order, with **loss of consciousness.**

---

**It is the COMMONEST manifestation** — the "typical fit" people picture.

---

## Step 2: The autonomic features

>> **Incontinence, apnoea, cyanosis.**

These matter because they carry **risk** — apnoea and cyanosis mean the child is not breathing during the fit, which is why a prolonged GTCS (status) is dangerous.

---

## Step 3: The EEG — match the trace to the phase

This is a favourite OSPE image. Two phases, two patterns:

>> **TONIC phase → fast spikes/polyspikes at 10–15 Hz**
>>
>> **CLONIC phase → diffuse high-voltage SLOW waves**

---

**Why does the EEG slow down in the clonic phase?**

In the tonic phase the whole cortex fires **fast and continuously** (stiffening). As the seizure progresses to clonic, the fast firing is **interrupted by pauses** (the relaxation between jerks) → the trace becomes **slow waves.**

>> **Fast spikes = continuous stiffening. Slow waves = the jerk-and-pause of clonus.** The EEG mirrors the movements.

---

## Step 4: Now the two FOCAL types — pair them by AWARENESS

| | Focal, aware (simple partial) | Focal, impaired (temporal/complex) |
|---|---|---|
| **Consciousness** | **INTACT** | **IMPAIRED** |
| **Duration** | **10–20 SECONDS** | **1–2 MINUTES** |
| **Automatisms** | **NONE** | **50–70%** |
| **Postictal phase** | **NONE** | **YES** |
| **Aura** | ± | yes (indicates focal onset) |

---

>> **The whole contrast is: aware/short/clean vs impaired/longer/messy.**

---

## Step 5: The two buzz-phrases to lock in

---

**VERSIVE seizure (in the aware type):**

>> **Head turning and conjugate eye movement** indicate a lesion in the **OPPOSITE frontal lobe.**

The eyes and head turn **away** from the side of the lesion. If they turn to the right, the focus is in the **left** frontal lobe.

---

**AUTOMATISMS (in the impaired-awareness type):**

>> Automatic, semi-purposeful behaviours — lip-smacking, chewing, fumbling — in **50–70%** of temporal lobe seizures.

The child is **not aware** and will not remember them.

---

## Step 6: The AURA — what it tells you

An **aura** (a warning sensation) means the seizure **started focally**, in a specific brain region, before spreading.

>> **An aura = focal onset.** A generalized seizure from the start has no aura, because there is no single focus to warn from.

And a focal seizure can **spread** to become a GTCS — **"focal to bilateral tonic-clonic"** (old secondary generalization).

---

## Putting it all together

**GTCS = stiffen (tonic) then jerk (clonic) with loss of consciousness**, the commonest type, with **EEG showing fast spikes (10–15 Hz) then slow waves** — mirroring the movements.

The two **focal** types split on **awareness**: **aware = short (10–20 s), no automatisms, no postictal**; **impaired awareness (temporal) = longer (1–2 min), automatisms in 50–70%, postictal phase.**

Two buzz-phrases: **versive seizure → lesion in the opposite frontal lobe**, and **automatisms → temporal lobe.** And an **aura always signals focal onset.**
cards.tie: The tonic-clonic seizure is the commonest and most examined type, and its two-phase EEG is a classic pictorial OSPE. The awareness-based contrast between the focal types is the practical application of the 2017 classification, and the versive-seizure localising sign is exactly the kind of detail that distinguishes a strong answer.

cards.q: Describe the absence seizure, including its clinical features and its characteristic EEG.
cards.lecturer: Absence seizure (simple or typical): sudden cessation of activity with a blank facial expression, lasts less than 30 seconds, plus or minus countless seizures per day. Age of onset is 5 years, more in girls. There is no loss of tone and no postictal phase, as the child resumes preseizure activity. Plus or minus automatism. Atypical absence has motor activity and loss of tone. EEG: 3 per second (3/sec) spike and generalized wave discharge.
cards.imgCaption: EEG of a typical absence seizure — the characteristic 3 Hz spike-and-wave discharge
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Picture the seizure

>> The child **suddenly stops**, **stares blankly**, then — just as suddenly — **carries on** as if nothing happened.

Lasts **under 30 seconds.** Can happen **countless times a day.**

---

## Step 2: The features that DEFINE it — and what they rule out

Two negatives are the giveaway:

>> **NO loss of tone** — the child does **not fall.**
>>
>> **NO postictal phase** — the child **immediately resumes** the previous activity, with no drowsiness or confusion.

---

**Why do these two negatives matter so much?**

Because they **distinguish absence from every other seizure type.**

- A **GTCS** causes falling and a long postictal phase
- An **absence** causes neither

>> **The child was talking, went blank for 10 seconds, and continued the sentence.** That story is absence and nothing else.

---

## Step 3: The demographics

>> **Age of onset ~5 years. More common in GIRLS.**

A school-age girl, typically.

---

## Step 4: The classic history — this is how it presents

The child is brought because they are **"daydreaming"** or **"not concentrating"** at school.

>> **Teachers, not doctors, usually notice absence seizures first** — because the child keeps "switching off" in class.

A child labelled inattentive who has **countless brief blank spells** may have **absence epilepsy**, not a behavioural problem.

---

## Step 5: The EEG — memorise this number exactly

>> **3 per second (3 Hz) spike-and-wave, generalized.**

This is **the** most examined EEG number in the whole topic.

---

**Why "generalized"?**

Because absence is a **generalized** seizure — both hemispheres discharge synchronously. So the spike-wave appears **all over** the trace at once, at a regular **3 per second** rhythm.

---

## Step 6: Typical vs atypical — the difference

| | Typical | Atypical |
|---|---|---|
| Tone | **preserved** (no fall) | **lost** (motor activity) |
| EEG | clean **3 Hz** spike-wave | slower, irregular |

>> **Typical = clean, 3 Hz, no motor signs. Atypical = motor activity and loss of tone.**

---

## Step 7: The treatment link

Absence responds to specific drugs:

>> **Ethosuximide** (drug of choice for typical absence) or **sodium valproate.**

**Note what is NOT used:** carbamazepine and phenytoin can **worsen** absence seizures — a favourite exam trap.

---

## Putting it all together

Absence = **sudden blank stare, < 30 seconds, no falling, no postictal phase, immediate resumption** — often in a **school-age girl** reported as "daydreaming."

The EEG is **3 Hz generalized spike-and-wave** — the number to memorise.

**Typical** preserves tone; **atypical** has motor activity and loss of tone.

Treat with **ethosuximide or valproate** — and remember that **carbamazepine can make it worse.**
cards.tie: Absence seizures are commonly missed because they look like daydreaming, so recognising the pattern from a school report is genuinely useful. The 3 Hz spike-and-wave EEG is one of the most examined facts in neurology, and knowing that ethosuximide and valproate are used while carbamazepine worsens absence protects you from a classic prescribing error.

cards.q: Describe infantile spasms and West syndrome, including the EEG and prognosis.
cards.lecturer: Infantile spasm (IS): age of onset 4-8 months; types are flexion, extension (least common) and mixed (commonest); lasts less than 1 second with several episodes per day; cause is 80-90% symptomatic (pre/peri/postnatal cause) and 10-20% cryptogenic; 80-90% risk of mental retardation. EEG shows hypsarrhythmia - a chaotic pattern of high voltage, bilaterally asynchronous, slow wave activity. West syndrome is the triad of infantile spasms, mental retardation and hypsarrhythmia.
cards.imgCaption: EEG of hypsarrhythmia — the chaotic, high-voltage, disorganized pattern of infantile spasms
cards.explain:
## Step 1: This is the SERIOUS one — flag it immediately

Unlike absence (benign) or febrile seizures (benign), infantile spasms carry a **grave prognosis.**

>> **80–90% risk of mental retardation.**

That number alone tells you this is a neurological emergency, not a benign event.

---

## Step 2: The demographics — a specific age

>> **Age of onset 4–8 months.**

An **infant**, not a toddler or school child. The age is a strong clue on its own.

---

## Step 3: What the spasms look like

>> **Types: flexion, extension (least common), MIXED (commonest).**

>> Each spasm lasts **< 1 second**, occurring in **clusters** — several episodes.

---

**The classic description:** a sudden brief **flexion of the neck, trunk and limbs** (a "jack-knife" or "salaam" spasm), in runs, often on waking.

>> Because each is so brief and they come in clusters, parents often think the baby is having **colic or startles** — and the diagnosis is delayed.

---

## Step 4: The cause split

>> **80–90% symptomatic** (a pre-, peri- or postnatal cause — a real brain problem)
>>
>> **10–20% cryptogenic** (no cause found)

The high symptomatic proportion is **why** the prognosis is poor — most have **underlying brain pathology.**

---

## Step 5: The EEG — the buzzword you MUST know

>> **HYPSARRHYTHMIA** — a **chaotic** pattern of **high-voltage, bilaterally asynchronous slow-wave** activity.

---

**Break the word down:** *hypsi-* = high, *-rhythmia* = rhythm. **High, chaotic rhythm.**

The normal organized EEG is **completely disorganized** — high voltage, random, both sides out of step.

>> **A chaotic, disorganized, high-voltage EEG in an infant with spasms = hypsarrhythmia = infantile spasms.**

---

## Step 6: WEST SYNDROME — the triad

>> **1. Infantile Spasms**
>> **2. Mental retardation**
>> **3. Hypsarrhythmia**

---

**Memory hook:** **West = Spasms + Slow development + Scrambled EEG.**

All three together = West syndrome.

---

## Step 7: The treatment link — this drug is unique to this condition

>> **ACTH** is the treatment for infantile spasms.

It is the **only** condition in the whole drug table treated with ACTH. Its side effects — **high glucose, high BP, cushingoid facies** — reflect its steroid-like action.

(Vigabatrin is also used, especially in tuberous sclerosis.)

---

## Step 8: Why early recognition matters so much

>> The **developmental outcome depends on early treatment.** Every week of untreated hypsarrhythmia worsens the prognosis.

An infant with clusters of brief spasms needs an **urgent EEG** — do not dismiss it as colic.

---

## Putting it all together

Infantile spasms: **age 4–8 months**, brief (**< 1 sec**) **mixed** flexion-extension spasms in **clusters**, mostly **symptomatic**, with an **80–90% risk of mental retardation.**

The EEG is **hypsarrhythmia** — a chaotic, high-voltage, disorganized trace.

**West syndrome = spasms + mental retardation + hypsarrhythmia.**

Treatment is **ACTH** — and early recognition is critical because outcome depends on prompt treatment.
cards.tie: Infantile spasms are a neurological emergency where early treatment genuinely changes the developmental outcome, yet they are frequently mistaken for colic. Recognising the triad of West syndrome, the hypsarrhythmia EEG, and the use of ACTH is high-yield for exams and important enough that missing it in practice has lifelong consequences for the child.

cards.q: Why is the diagnosis of epilepsy described as 'ultimately clinical', and what does the workup add?
cards.lecturer: The diagnosis of epilepsy is ultimately clinical. It is based on a detailed description of events experienced by the patient or an eyewitness account. The elements of diagnosis involve determining: is it epilepsy or are the seizures related to an acute encephalopathy; if epilepsy, the seizure/syndrome needs to be categorized; and any identifiable treatable aetiology. Investigations include FBC/ESR, RBS, S/E/U/Cr including Ca and Mg, anticonvulsant blood levels, blood and urine amino acids, urine organic acids, EEG including awake and sleep tracings, EEG special studies (24-hr ambulatory, video-EEG, all-night recording), ECG if cardiac disorder suspected, skull X-rays if abnormal head shape/size, neuroimaging (CT/MRI, PET, SPECT), and LP if infection is suspected and there are no contraindications.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: The most important sentence in the diagnosis slide

>> **"The diagnosis of epilepsy is ultimately CLINICAL."**

This surprises students who expect the EEG to make the diagnosis. **It does not.**

---

## Step 2: Why clinical, not the EEG?

Because:

- A person with epilepsy can have a **NORMAL EEG** between seizures (you rarely catch the seizure on the trace)
- A person **without** epilepsy can have **minor EEG abnormalities**

---

>> **The EEG supports the diagnosis; it does not make it.** A normal EEG does not exclude epilepsy, and an abnormal EEG alone does not confirm it.

---

## Step 3: So what DOES make the diagnosis?

>> **A detailed description of the event** — from the patient or, better, an **EYEWITNESS.**

The child is unconscious during most seizures, so **someone who saw it** is often your best source.

---

**What you want to know:** what happened before (aura?), during (stiffening, jerking, blank stare, automatisms?), and after (drowsy postictal phase or immediate recovery?).

>> **The story tells you the seizure type. The seizure type guides the drug.**

---

## Step 4: The three questions the diagnosis must answer

>> **1. Is it EPILEPSY, or an acute ENCEPHALOPATHY?**
>>
>> **2. If epilepsy, CATEGORIZE the seizure/syndrome**
>>
>> **3. Find any TREATABLE aetiology**

---

**Question 1 is a safety question.** An **acute encephalopathy** — meningitis, metabolic crisis, poisoning — is a **different, urgent** problem. You must not label an acutely encephalopathic child as "epileptic."

**Question 3 is the one that can cure.** A **treatable** cause — hypoglycaemia, hypocalcaemia, meningitis — must be found, because treating it stops the seizures at the source.

---

## Step 5: What the investigations ADD (they support, not replace)

**Find a treatable cause:**

- **RBS** (glucose), **electrolytes with Ca and Mg** — the metabolic causes
- **Amino/organic acids** — inborn errors of metabolism
- **LP** — infection

**Characterize and localize:**

- **EEG** (awake AND asleep; video-EEG; ambulatory) — supports classification
- **Neuroimaging (CT/MRI)** — finds a structural lesion

**Monitor and exclude mimics:**

- **Anticonvulsant blood levels** — check compliance/toxicity
- **ECG** — exclude a cardiac cause (long QT) masquerading as seizures

---

## Step 6: Two conditions on the investigations worth noting

>> **EEG "awake AND sleep"** — some discharges (e.g. Rolandic) only appear in **sleep**, so a single awake EEG can miss them.

>> **LP only "if there are no contraindications"** — raised intracranial pressure contraindicates LP because of the risk of coning.

---

## Putting it all together

Epilepsy is diagnosed **clinically**, from a **detailed eyewitness description** — because the EEG can be normal in epilepsy and abnormal without it.

The workup answers three questions: **is it epilepsy or an acute encephalopathy, what seizure/syndrome is it, and is there a treatable cause?**

Investigations **support** this: **glucose, electrolytes, Ca/Mg, LP** find treatable causes; **EEG (awake and asleep) and imaging** characterize and localize; and **ECG** excludes a cardiac mimic.

**The story makes the diagnosis; the tests refine and confirm it.**
cards.tie: Understanding that epilepsy is a clinical diagnosis stops you from over-relying on the EEG and from wrongly reassuring a family because 'the EEG was normal'. The emphasis on finding a treatable cause is what turns a seizure workup from labelling into potentially curing, since correcting hypoglycaemia, hypocalcaemia or meningitis addresses the seizure at its source.

cards.q: List the seizure mimics, and explain why an ECG is essential in a child with collapse.
cards.lecturer: Conditions that mimic seizures share common features with epilepsy such as sudden onset of abnormal consciousness, behaviour, posture, tone, sensation or autonomic function. Seizure mimics include: breath-holding spells (cyanotic and pallid), gastro-oesophageal reflux in small infants, benign paroxysmal vertigo, syncope (simple and cough) which resembles the atonic type, night terrors, tics, narcolepsy, cataplexy, sleep apnoea, hypoglycaemia, parasomnias (night terrors, sleep walking, sleep talking, nocturnal enuresis), prolonged QT syndrome which is characterized by sudden loss of consciousness during exercise or an emotional and stressful experience, and pseudoseizures/hysterical seizures (psychogenic non-epileptic seizures, PNES, of psychological cause).
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Why do these conditions get confused with seizures?

>> Because they **share a sudden onset** of abnormal **consciousness, behaviour, posture, tone, sensation or autonomic function.**

Anything that causes a child to suddenly collapse, jerk, stare, or behave oddly **can look like a fit** — but isn't one.

---

## Step 2: Group the mimics so they are memorable

---

**Group A — Breathing-related (infants and toddlers)**

- **Breath-holding spells** — cyanotic and pallid
- **Sleep apnoea**

---

**Group B — Circulation-related (the dangerous group)**

- **Syncope** — simple and cough (**resembles the atonic seizure**)
- **PROLONGED QT SYNDROME** — collapse on exercise/emotion

---

**Group C — Sleep-related (parasomnias)**

- **Night terrors, sleep-walking, sleep-talking, nocturnal enuresis**
- **Narcolepsy, cataplexy**

---

**Group D — Other**

- **Gastro-oesophageal reflux** (in small infants — Sandifer's posturing)
- **Benign paroxysmal vertigo**
- **Tics**
- **Hypoglycaemia**
- **Pseudoseizures / PNES** — psychological, not electrical

---

## Step 3: Now the one that can KILL — long QT syndrome

This is the single most important mimic to recognise.

>> **Prolonged QT syndrome** causes **sudden loss of consciousness during EXERCISE or emotional/stressful experience.**

---

**Why is it deadly?**

Long QT is a **cardiac arrhythmia** disorder. The "collapse" is not a seizure — it is the heart going into a **dangerous rhythm (torsades de pointes)** that can degenerate into **cardiac arrest and sudden death.**

---

>> **If you misdiagnose long QT as epilepsy and start an anti-seizure drug, you have missed a killer — and the child may die during the next episode.**

---

## Step 4: The rule that saves lives

>> **ALWAYS do an ECG in a child who collapses — especially collapse triggered by EXERCISE or EMOTION.**

The ECG shows the prolonged QT interval. This costs nothing and takes minutes, and it separates a benign misdiagnosis from a fatal one.

---

**The trigger is the clue:** epilepsy is usually **not** triggered by exercise. **Collapse ON exertion points to the heart, not the brain.**

---

## Step 5: The other mimic worth understanding — pseudoseizures (PNES)

>> **Psychogenic non-epileptic seizures** — the cause is **psychological**, not electrical.

The events look like seizures but there is **no epileptic discharge on EEG** during them. They are real distress, not fabrication, and need **psychological**, not anticonvulsant, management.

>> **Video-EEG** (catching an event with no EEG change) is how PNES is confirmed.

---

## Step 6: Why this whole slide matters

>> **Not everything that shakes or collapses is epilepsy.**

Misdiagnosing a mimic as epilepsy means: an unnecessary lifelong label, unnecessary drugs, and — with long QT — **a missed fatal condition.**

---

## Putting it all together

Seizure mimics share a **sudden onset of altered consciousness, tone, behaviour or autonomic function** but are **not** epileptic.

Group them: **breathing** (breath-holding, apnoea), **circulation** (syncope, long QT), **sleep** (parasomnias, narcolepsy), and **other** (reflux, vertigo, tics, hypoglycaemia, pseudoseizures).

The one that can kill is **prolonged QT syndrome** — collapse on **exercise or emotion** — which is why you **always do an ECG in a child with collapse.**

And **pseudoseizures** are psychological, confirmed by a normal EEG during an event, and need psychological care.
cards.tie: Distinguishing seizures from their mimics prevents both over-diagnosis of epilepsy and the catastrophic error of missing long QT syndrome, which can cause sudden death. The habit of doing an ECG in any child with unexplained collapse, particularly exertional collapse, is simple, cheap and genuinely life-saving.

cards.q: Outline the stepwise treatment of seizures and match the key antiepileptic drugs to their seizure types and side effects.
cards.lecturer: Stepwise treatment: Step 1 - ABC of resuscitation (airway, ventilation, cardiac function, TPR, BP, RBS, quick history); Step 2 - abort seizure using the status epilepticus guideline; Step 3 - detailed evaluation to elicit cause; Step 4 - treatment of an identifiable cause; Step 5 - for epilepsy or epileptic syndrome, place on appropriate ASM and continue follow-up. Key drugs: Phenobarbitone (GTC, partial; opens Cl channel via GABA receptor; side effects hyperkinesia, drowsiness); Phenytoin (GTC, partial, status; blocks Na channels; gum hypertrophy, acne, ataxia, nystagmus); Carbamazepine (GTC, partial; same as phenytoin; rashes, ataxia, low Hb, neutropenia, hepatotoxicity); Ethosuximide (typical absence; blocks Ca channel of thalamocortical circuitry; vomiting, rashes); Na valproate (broad spectrum - GTC, absence, myoclonic; nausea, drowsiness, tremor); ACTH (infantile spasm); Benzodiazepines (status epilepticus).
cards.explain:
## Step 1: The five steps — and Step 1 is non-negotiable

>> **Step 1: ABC of resuscitation — ALWAYS FIRST**

A seizing child may not be breathing (apnoea, cyanosis). **Before any drug**, secure the airway, check breathing and circulation, and check the **blood sugar.**

---

>> **Step 2: Abort** the seizure (status guideline)
>> **Step 3: Evaluate** for the cause
>> **Step 4: Treat** the cause
>> **Step 5: Long-term** ASM + neurology follow-up

---

**Why check RBS in Step 1?** Because **hypoglycaemia** is a rapidly treatable cause — glucose can stop the seizure at its source, before any anticonvulsant.

---

## Step 2: The drug table becomes logical if you use the see-saw

Recall: **glutamate = accelerator, GABA = brake.** Every drug pushes toward the brake.

| Drug | Mechanism | See-saw action |
|---|---|---|
| **Phenobarbitone** | opens Cl⁻ channel via **GABA** receptor | **boost the brake** |
| **Benzodiazepines** | enhance **GABA** Cl⁻ channel opening | **boost the brake** |
| **Phenytoin** | blocks **Na⁺** channels | **cut the accelerator** |
| **Carbamazepine** | blocks **Na⁺** channels (same as phenytoin) | **cut the accelerator** |
| **Valproate** | blocks Na⁺ + K⁺ conductance | **cut the accelerator (broad)** |
| **Ethosuximide** | blocks **Ca²⁺** channels of thalamocortical circuit | absence-specific |

---

## Step 3: Match the drug to the SEIZURE — the high-yield pairs

>> **Typical absence → ETHOSUXIMIDE** (or valproate)
>>
>> **Infantile spasm → ACTH**
>>
>> **Rolandic epilepsy → CARBAMAZEPINE**
>>
>> **Broad spectrum (many types) → SODIUM VALPROATE**
>>
>> **Status epilepticus first line → BENZODIAZEPINE**
>>
>> **Status second line → PHENYTOIN**

---

>> These specific pairings are exactly what examiners ask. Learn them as fixed couples.

---

## Step 4: The side effects examiners love

---

>> **PHENYTOIN → GUM HYPERTROPHY** (plus acne, ataxia, nystagmus)

**Gum hypertrophy is the classic one** — a child on long-term phenytoin with overgrown gums is a giveaway.

---

>> **CARBAMAZEPINE → blood problems** (low Hb, neutropenia) and **hepatotoxicity**, plus rashes

---

>> **PHENOBARBITONE → hyperkinesia and drowsiness** — note the paradox: it can make children **hyperactive.**

---

>> **ACTH → high glucose, high BP, cushingoid facies** — the steroid-like effects.

---

>> **BENZODIAZEPINES → CNS depression / respiratory depression** — critical in status, where you must watch the breathing.

---

## Step 5: Why ethosuximide is unusual

It is the **only** drug acting on **thalamocortical Ca²⁺ channels** — and it is **absence-specific.**

>> Absence seizures are generated by a **thalamocortical circuit**, so a drug targeting that circuit's calcium channels works precisely there.

**This is why ethosuximide treats absence but not tonic-clonic seizures** — it hits the specific circuit involved.

---

## Step 6: A caution the table implies

Note that **carbamazepine and phenytoin** (Na⁺-channel blockers) treat **GTC and partial** seizures — but they can **worsen absence.**

>> **Match the drug to the seizure. The wrong drug can make a different seizure type worse.**

---

## Putting it all together

Treatment runs **ABC first (with glucose) → abort → evaluate → treat cause → long-term ASM.**

The mechanisms follow the **see-saw**: **phenobarbitone and benzodiazepines boost GABA (brake)**; **phenytoin, carbamazepine and valproate block Na⁺ (cut the accelerator)**; **ethosuximide blocks thalamocortical Ca²⁺** for absence.

Learn the **drug-seizure couples**: **ethosuximide-absence, ACTH-spasms, carbamazepine-Rolandic, valproate-broad, benzo-status.**

And the **side-effect flags**: **phenytoin-gum hypertrophy, carbamazepine-marrow/liver, phenobarbitone-hyperkinesia, ACTH-cushingoid, benzo-respiratory depression.**
cards.tie: The drug table is dense but examiners reliably ask two things — which drug for which seizure, and the classic side effect of each. Phenytoin-induced gum hypertrophy and the use of ACTH for infantile spasms are perennial favourites, and knowing that the wrong drug can worsen absence seizures is a genuine prescribing safeguard.

cards.q: Define status epilepticus (old and new), explain why the definition changed, and give the management ladder.
cards.lecturer: Status epilepticus. Old definition: a life-threatening seizure prolonged for more than 30 minutes, or repeated seizures with no recovery of consciousness in between the attacks. Current definition: a seizure that lasts longer than 5 minutes, or having more than one seizure within a 5 minute period without returning to a normal level of consciousness between episodes. Generalized tonic-clonic is most alarming because of the risk of hypoxia. Management: ABC of resuscitation, blood sugar estimation, IV line and blood sample. First line - benzodiazepines (IV diazepam 0.1-0.3mg/kg max 3 doses, IV lorazepam 0.05-0.1mg/kg, or rectal diazepam/buccal/nasal midazolam for difficult IV access). Second line if seizure persists - phenytoin 15-20mg/kg (fosphenytoin preferred, ECG monitoring for arrhythmia), phenobarbital 15-20mg/kg, sodium valproate IV, levetiracetam. Third line - ICU care with induction of anaesthesia, intubation, mechanical ventilation, continuous EEG and cardiopulmonary monitoring.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Learn BOTH definitions — the change itself is examined

>> **OLD:** a seizure prolonged **> 30 minutes**, or repeated seizures without recovery of consciousness.
>>
>> **NEW (use this one):** a seizure lasting **> 5 minutes**, or repeated seizures within 5 minutes **without returning to normal consciousness** between them.

---

## Step 2: WHY did the definition shrink from 30 to 5 minutes?

This is the reasoning examiners want.

>> **Most seizures stop on their own within 2–3 minutes.**

So a seizure still going at **5 minutes** is behaving abnormally — it will **probably not stop by itself** and needs treatment **now.**

---

**The old 30-minute rule was dangerous** because:

- Waiting 30 minutes to act means **30 minutes of hypoxia and neuronal injury**
- Recall: **seizures beget seizures** — the longer it runs, the harder it is to stop and the more damage it causes

---

>> **The definition changed to make you ACT SOONER.** Five minutes is the practical trigger to start drugs.

---

## Step 3: Why is GTCS the most alarming?

>> Because of the **risk of HYPOXIA.**

During a generalized tonic-clonic seizure the child has **apnoea and cyanosis** — they are **not breathing.** Prolonged, this causes brain injury and can be fatal.

---

## Step 4: The management ladder — three lines

Start, as always, with **ABC + blood sugar + IV access.**

---

**FIRST LINE — BENZODIAZEPINES**

>> IV **diazepam** 0.1–0.3 mg/kg (max 3 doses), or IV **lorazepam** 0.05–0.1 mg/kg.
>>
>> **No IV access?** → **rectal diazepam** 0.2–0.5 mg/kg, or **buccal/nasal midazolam** 0.5 mg/kg.

**Watch for: respiratory depression and hypotension.**

---

**SECOND LINE (seizure persists) — the loading agents**

>> **Phenytoin** 15–20 mg/kg (**fosphenytoin preferred**; monitor ECG for **arrhythmia**)
>> **Phenobarbital** 15–20 mg/kg
>> **Sodium valproate** IV
>> **Levetiracetam**

---

**THIRD LINE — ICU / ANAESTHESIA**

>> Induce anaesthesia (**thiopental, midazolam, propofol**), **intubate and ventilate**, with **continuous EEG and cardiopulmonary monitoring.**

---

## Step 5: Two safety points in the drug choices

>> **Buccal/nasal midazolam** is a lifesaver **when there is no IV access** — hugely relevant in a resource-limited or pre-hospital setting.

>> **Phenytoin needs ECG monitoring** because it can cause **arrhythmia**; **fosphenytoin** is preferred as it is safer to infuse.

---

## Step 6: The aetiology to remember

Common causes of status: **non-compliance with drugs**, **febrile seizure**, **intercurrent infection**, and brain pathology.

>> **Non-compliance is a leading, preventable cause.** A known epileptic in status has often **stopped their medication** — always ask.

---

## Putting it all together

Status = a seizure **> 5 minutes** (or repeated without recovery) — the definition **dropped from 30 to 5 minutes** so that you **treat sooner**, because most seizures self-terminate by 3 minutes and prolonged seizing causes hypoxia and injury.

**GTCS is most alarming** because of **hypoxia** from apnoea.

Manage in a ladder: **ABC + glucose → benzodiazepine (first line) → phenytoin/phenobarbital/valproate/levetiracetam (second) → ICU anaesthesia (third).**

Use **buccal/nasal midazolam if no IV**, monitor **ECG with phenytoin**, and always ask about **drug non-compliance.**
cards.tie: Status epilepticus is a true emergency you will manage on call, and the shift to the 5-minute definition exists precisely to make you act quickly. Knowing the benzodiazepine-first ladder, the buccal midazolam option when there is no IV access, and that non-compliance is a leading cause equips you to treat it correctly and fast.

cards.q: Give the definition and classification of febrile seizures, and outline the management including when to do a lumbar puncture.
cards.lecturer: Febrile seizures occur in 2-5% of neurologically healthy infants and children, commoner in males, with a hereditary tendency. Classification: simple febrile seizure is brief (lasting <15 minutes), usually a single episode in 24 hours, and generalized; complex febrile seizure lasts >15 minutes, is usually multiple in 24 hours, and is focal. Before diagnosis, rule out intracranial infection. Management: ABCD of resuscitation, control the temperature with antipyretics, abort the seizure with diazepam (IV/rectal, 0.1-0.3mg/kg given IV slowly to avoid respiratory arrest) or intramuscular paraldehyde (0.1ml/kg). Investigate for the cause: FBC, blood film for malaria parasite, and lumbar puncture for CSF analysis to rule out meningitis, especially in children <18 months who don't show the usual signs. Treat the underlying cause and counsel parents that the disorder is benign but can recur.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: The frequency and who gets them

>> **2–5% of neurologically healthy children**, commoner in **males**, with a **hereditary** tendency.

Note **"neurologically healthy"** — these are otherwise **normal** children. That is part of why the prognosis is good.

---

## Step 2: Simple vs complex — THREE questions

This is the most examined part. Ask three things:

| Question | Simple | Complex |
|---|---|---|
| **How long?** | **< 15 min** | **> 15 min** |
| **How many in 24 h?** | **single** | **multiple** |
| **Focal or generalized?** | **generalized** | **focal** |

---

>> **Simple = Short, Single, generalized (Symmetrical). Complex = the opposite of each.**

**Complex febrile seizures matter more** because they carry a **higher risk of later epilepsy** (up to 29% for focal, vs 1% for simple).

---

## Step 3: The rule that must come FIRST

>> **Before diagnosing a febrile seizure, RULE OUT intracranial infection.**

Remember the definition itself **excludes CNS infection.** So you cannot make the diagnosis until you have excluded **meningitis and encephalitis.**

---

## Step 4: Management — treat the seizure, then find the cause

**Abort the seizure:**

>> **Diazepam** (IV or rectal, 0.1–0.3 mg/kg) — **give IV slowly to avoid respiratory arrest**
>>
>> or **IM paraldehyde** (0.1 ml/kg)

**Control the temperature** with antipyretics, alongside **ABCD.**

---

## Step 5: Investigate for the cause of the FEVER

>> **FBC**, **blood film for malaria parasites** (crucial in our setting), and **lumbar puncture** for CSF.

Common fever sources: **malaria, URTI, otitis media, pharyngitis.**

---

## Step 6: The LP rule — this is the exam's favourite point

>> Do an **LP, especially in children UNDER 18 MONTHS**, to rule out meningitis.

---

**Why specifically under 18 months?**

>> Because at that age, meningitis may **NOT show the usual signs** — neck stiffness and Kernig's/Brudzinski's signs are **unreliable in infants.**

An infant with meningitis can look just like a child with a simple febrile seizure. **The only way to be sure is the LP.**

---

>> **The younger the child, the lower your threshold for LP** — because the clinical signs of meningitis fail you at that age.

---

## Step 7: Counselling — a key part of management

>> Reassure parents the disorder is **BENIGN** but **can RECUR.**

Teach them **home fever management** and what to do if a seizure occurs. This reassurance is genuinely therapeutic — parents are often terrified they have watched their child nearly die.

---

## Step 8: The reassuring statistic

>> After a **simple** febrile seizure, the risk of later epilepsy is only **~1%** — barely above the background population risk.

The risk rises with **complex/focal** features, **family history of epilepsy**, and **neurodevelopmental abnormality.**

---

## Putting it all together

Febrile seizures affect **2–5% of neurologically healthy children**, more in **boys**, and run in families.

**Simple = short (<15 min), single, generalized. Complex = long, multiple, focal** — and complex carries the higher epilepsy risk.

**Always exclude intracranial infection first**, treat with **diazepam or paraldehyde** plus antipyretics, and **do an LP especially under 18 months** because meningitis signs are unreliable at that age.

Then **reassure the parents** — it is benign, though it can recur, with only a **1% epilepsy risk** after a simple febrile seizure.
cards.tie: Febrile seizures are the commonest seizure you will manage, and the single most important lesson is to exclude meningitis before reassuring the family — with a low threshold for LP under 18 months where the classic signs are absent. The simple-versus-complex classification and the reassuring epilepsy statistics are exactly what is examined and what parents need to hear.

cards.q: CLINICAL SCENARIO: An 18-month-old is brought fitting, having had a fever from malaria. The generalized convulsion has now lasted 8 minutes and continues. Outline your immediate management.
cards.lecturer: This is status epilepticus (a seizure lasting longer than 5 minutes) occurring in the context of a febrile illness (malaria). Immediate management: Step 1 - ABC of resuscitation: secure the airway, give oxygen, check breathing and circulation, and crucially check the blood glucose (RBS). Step 2 - abort the seizure: first-line is a benzodiazepine - IV diazepam 0.1-0.3mg/kg (max 3 doses) or IV lorazepam 0.05-0.1mg/kg slowly; if IV access is difficult, rectal diazepam 0.2-0.5mg/kg or buccal/nasal midazolam 0.5mg/kg. Watch for respiratory depression. If the seizure persists, second-line agents: phenytoin/fosphenytoin 15-20mg/kg with ECG monitoring, phenobarbital, valproate or levetiracetam. Control the temperature with antipyretics. Investigate: FBC, blood film for malaria parasites, electrolytes with Ca and Mg, and lumbar puncture to exclude meningitis (especially important as the child is under 18 months where meningeal signs are unreliable) - provided there are no contraindications.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Recognise TWO things at once

**First:** the seizure has lasted **8 minutes and continues** →

>> **This is STATUS EPILEPTICUS** (> 5 minutes by the current definition).

**Second:** there is a **fever from malaria** → this is happening in a **febrile** context, and the child is **under 18 months.**

Both facts shape your management.

---

## Step 2: Step 1 — ABC, and do NOT forget the glucose

>> **Airway, Breathing, Circulation — with oxygen.**

A child in a prolonged GTCS is **apnoeic and cyanosed** — the airway and oxygen come first.

---

>> **Check the blood glucose (RBS).**

**Hypoglycaemia** is a rapidly reversible cause. In a sick, febrile, possibly poorly-feeding child with malaria, **hypoglycaemia is common** — and giving glucose can stop the seizure at its source.

---

## Step 3: Step 2 — abort with a BENZODIAZEPINE (first line)

>> **IV diazepam 0.1–0.3 mg/kg** (max 3 doses), or **IV lorazepam 0.05–0.1 mg/kg** slowly.

**No IV access** (likely in a small fitting child)?

>> **Rectal diazepam 0.2–0.5 mg/kg**, or **buccal/nasal midazolam 0.5 mg/kg.**

**Watch for respiratory depression** — have airway support ready.

---

## Step 4: If it persists — second line

>> **Phenytoin/fosphenytoin 15–20 mg/kg** (fosphenytoin preferred; **ECG monitoring** for arrhythmia), or **phenobarbital, valproate, levetiracetam.**

If it still continues → **third line: ICU, anaesthesia, intubation.**

---

## Step 5: Treat the fever and the malaria

>> **Antipyretics** to control the temperature, and **antimalarial treatment** for the underlying malaria — a treatable cause driving the seizure.

Recall: **malaria is a listed common cause of the fever** in febrile seizures, and **cerebral malaria** is a listed infective cause of seizures directly.

---

## Step 6: The critical investigation — LUMBAR PUNCTURE

This is where the age matters enormously.

>> **This child is UNDER 18 months.** Do an **LP to exclude meningitis** — provided there are **no contraindications** (e.g. raised ICP).

---

**Why is the LP so important here?**

>> Because **you cannot yet call this a febrile seizure.** The definition **requires excluding CNS infection.**

And **under 18 months, meningitis may not show the usual signs** — no neck stiffness, no Kernig's sign.

>> **A child with meningitis can look identical to a febrile seizure at this age. The LP is the only way to be sure.**

Do not be falsely reassured that "it's just a febrile seizure from malaria" — **it could be cerebral malaria OR meningitis**, and both are dangerous.

---

## Step 7: The other investigations

>> **FBC, blood film for malaria parasites** (confirm the malaria), **electrolytes with Ca and Mg** (exclude metabolic causes), and glucose.

---

## Step 8: Do not forget — is this even a simple febrile seizure?

At **8 minutes** it is already heading toward **complex** (> 15 min) and it is **prolonged**.

>> A prolonged febrile seizure has a **higher risk of subsequent epilepsy** and warrants closer follow-up.

---

## Putting it all together

This is **status epilepticus in a febrile, under-18-month child** — treat it as **both.**

**ABC + oxygen + glucose first** (hypoglycaemia is common and reversible), then **abort with a benzodiazepine** (buccal midazolam if no IV), escalating through **phenytoin → ICU** if it persists.

Treat the **fever and the malaria**, and — crucially — **do an LP to exclude meningitis**, because **under 18 months the signs are unreliable and you cannot call it a febrile seizure until CNS infection is excluded.**
cards.tie: This scenario combines the two commonest seizure emergencies in our setting — status epilepticus and febrile seizures with malaria — and tests whether you resuscitate first, check glucose, use the benzodiazepine ladder, and remember the under-18-month LP rule. Every element is directly applicable on a Nigerian paediatric ward and is exactly what examiners construct these questions to test.

cards.q: OSPE TECHNIQUE: You are shown an EEG in a paediatric neurology station. How do you approach reading it, and what are the classic patterns you must recognise?
cards.lecturer: A systematic approach to a paediatric EEG: (1) Confirm it is an EEG and note the montage and calibration (voltage and time markers). (2) Assess the background rhythm - is it normal and organized for age, or abnormal/disorganized. (3) Identify any abnormal discharges - spikes, sharp waves, spike-and-wave complexes - and whether they are focal (one region) or generalized (both hemispheres). (4) Note the frequency of any repetitive discharge. (5) Correlate with the clinical picture. The classic patterns to recognise: 3 Hz (3 per second) spike-and-wave discharge = typical absence seizure; hypsarrhythmia (chaotic, high-voltage, bilaterally asynchronous slow waves) = infantile spasms/West syndrome; centrotemporal spikes on a normal background = benign Rolandic epilepsy; spikes and polyspikes at 10-15 Hz then diffuse slow waves = generalized tonic-clonic seizure; focal spikes and sharp waves = focal (partial) seizures.
cards.imgCaption: Two classic EEGs to recognise instantly — 3 Hz spike-and-wave of absence (left) and hypsarrhythmia of infantile spasms (right)
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Do NOT blurt out the diagnosis — read systematically

Just like a chest X-ray, an EEG has a system. Working through it earns marks and stops you jumping to the wrong answer.

---

## Step 2: The reading sequence

>> **1. Confirm it is an EEG** — note the **montage** and **calibration** (voltage and time markers)

>> **2. Background rhythm** — is it **normal and organized for age**, or **disorganized**?

>> **3. Abnormal discharges** — **spikes, sharp waves, spike-and-wave** complexes?

>> **4. Distribution** — **focal** (one region) or **generalized** (both hemispheres)?

>> **5. Frequency** — how many discharges per second?

>> **6. Correlate** with the clinical picture

---

## Step 3: The BACKGROUND is your first big clue

>> If the **background is chaotic and disorganized**, think **hypsarrhythmia** immediately.
>>
>> If the **background is NORMAL** with spikes on top, think **Rolandic epilepsy** or focal discharges.

---

## Step 4: The four classic patterns — learn each with its condition

---

**PATTERN 1 — 3 Hz spike-and-wave, generalized**

>> = **TYPICAL ABSENCE seizure**

Regular, symmetrical, **3 per second.** The most examined EEG number.

---

**PATTERN 2 — Hypsarrhythmia**

>> = **INFANTILE SPASMS / WEST SYNDROME**

**Chaotic, high-voltage, bilaterally asynchronous slow waves.** The background itself is destroyed. In an **infant**, this is the answer.

---

**PATTERN 3 — Centrotemporal spikes on a NORMAL background**

>> = **BENIGN ROLANDIC EPILEPSY**

The key is the **normal background** — only the centrotemporal spikes are abnormal. School-age child, nocturnal seizures.

---

**PATTERN 4 — Spikes/polyspikes 10–15 Hz, then diffuse slow waves**

>> = **GENERALIZED TONIC-CLONIC seizure**

Fast spikes (tonic phase) evolving to slow waves (clonic phase).

---

**PATTERN 5 — Focal spikes and sharp waves**

>> = **FOCAL (partial) seizures** — localized to one region.

---

## Step 5: Use AGE + EEG together

The pattern plus the child's age usually gives the diagnosis:

| Age | EEG | Diagnosis |
|---|---|---|
| **Infant (4–8 mo)** | hypsarrhythmia | **West syndrome** |
| **~5 yr girl** | 3 Hz spike-wave | **absence** |
| **School age, nocturnal** | centrotemporal spikes, normal background | **Rolandic** |

---

>> **The EEG rarely stands alone — pair it with the age and the story.**

---

## Step 6: The two traps

**Trap 1 — calling any spike "epilepsy."**

>> The EEG **supports**, it does not **make**, the diagnosis. A normal EEG does not exclude epilepsy; minor abnormalities do not confirm it.

**Trap 2 — ignoring the background.**

>> The **background** distinguishes Rolandic (normal) from serious generalized epilepsies (abnormal). Always comment on it.

---

## Step 7: How to present

>> "This is an EEG. The **background is [normal/disorganized]**. There are **[generalized/focal] [spike-and-wave/spikes]** discharges at **[frequency]**. This pattern of **[3 Hz spike-wave / hypsarrhythmia / centrotemporal spikes]** is consistent with **[absence / West syndrome / Rolandic epilepsy]**, which fits a child of this age presenting with **[the history]**."

---

## Putting it all together

Read the EEG **systematically**: calibration → **background** → discharges → **focal or generalized** → frequency → correlate.

Recognise the **four classic patterns**: **3 Hz spike-wave = absence; hypsarrhythmia = infantile spasms/West; centrotemporal spikes on normal background = Rolandic; 10–15 Hz spikes then slow waves = GTCS.**

**Pair the EEG with the age and story**, comment on the **background**, and remember the EEG **supports** rather than makes the diagnosis.
cards.tie: The EEG is the classic pictorial OSPE for paediatric neurology, and these four patterns cover almost everything you will be shown. Being able to read systematically, comment on the background, and pair the pattern with the child's age to reach a diagnosis is exactly what the station tests, and it prevents the common error of treating any abnormal EEG as proof of epilepsy.

cards.q: CLINICAL SCENARIO: A teacher reports that a 6-year-old girl frequently 'daydreams', switching off for a few seconds many times a day, then carrying on as if nothing happened, with no falling and no drowsiness afterwards. What is the diagnosis, how do you confirm it, and how do you treat it?
cards.lecturer: The diagnosis is typical absence seizures (childhood absence epilepsy). The features are classic: sudden cessation of activity with a blank stare, lasting less than 30 seconds, countless times a day, in a child around 5 years and more common in girls, with no loss of tone (no falling) and no postictal phase (she resumes preseizure activity immediately). Confirmation is by EEG, which shows the characteristic 3 per second (3 Hz) generalized spike-and-wave discharge; hyperventilation during the EEG can provoke an episode. Treatment is ethosuximide (drug of choice for typical absence) or sodium valproate. Carbamazepine and phenytoin should be avoided as they can worsen absence seizures. The prognosis is generally good, with many children outgrowing it.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Recognise the pattern from the story alone

Walk through what the teacher describes against the absence checklist:

- **"Switches off for a few seconds"** → sudden cessation, **< 30 sec** ✓
- **"Many times a day"** → countless seizures/day ✓
- **"Carries on as if nothing happened"** → **no postictal phase** ✓
- **"No falling"** → **no loss of tone** ✓
- **6-year-old girl** → onset ~5 years, more in **girls** ✓

---

>> **Every feature fits. This is typical absence (childhood absence epilepsy).**

---

## Step 2: The two negatives clinch it

>> **No falling. No drowsiness afterwards.**

These two negatives **exclude the other seizure types:**

- A **GTCS** would cause falling and a long postictal phase
- An **atonic** seizure would cause falling

>> **A brief blank spell with immediate full recovery is absence and nothing else.**

---

## Step 3: Why the TEACHER noticed first

This is a classic and important point.

>> Absence seizures are **so brief and undramatic** that they are usually first reported as **"daydreaming"** or **"inattention"** — by **teachers**, not parents.

A child labelled as inattentive or a poor concentrator, who has **countless brief switch-offs**, may have **absence epilepsy** rather than a behavioural problem.

>> **Do not dismiss "daydreaming" in a child — it can be a seizure.**

---

## Step 4: Confirm with the EEG

>> **EEG shows 3 Hz generalized spike-and-wave** — the diagnostic pattern.

---

**A neat bedside/EEG trick:**

>> **HYPERVENTILATION provokes absence seizures.** Asking the child to blow on a windmill or overbreathe during the EEG can **trigger a typical episode**, capturing it on the trace.

This is a genuinely useful and examinable point.

---

## Step 5: Treatment — and the trap

>> **Ethosuximide** (drug of choice for typical absence), or **sodium valproate.**

---

>> **AVOID carbamazepine and phenytoin — they can WORSEN absence seizures.**

This is a favourite exam trap. Both are excellent for tonic-clonic and focal seizures, but they can **exacerbate** absence.

**Match the drug to the seizure type** — the wrong choice here makes the child worse.

---

## Step 6: Prognosis — reassure

>> The prognosis is **generally good**, and **many children outgrow** absence epilepsy.

This fits the epidemiology: **many children outgrow epilepsy**, which is why prevalence (0.5–0.8%) is lower than lifetime incidence (3%).

---

## Step 7: Why ethosuximide works here specifically

Ethosuximide blocks **thalamocortical Ca²⁺ channels.**

>> Absence seizures are **generated by the thalamocortical circuit**, so a drug targeting that circuit's calcium channels works **precisely** on the mechanism.

---

## Putting it all together

**Brief blank stares, < 30 sec, countless per day, no falling, no postictal phase, in a ~5-year-old girl** = **typical absence seizures**, often first reported by a **teacher** as daydreaming.

Confirm with an **EEG showing 3 Hz spike-and-wave** — provoked by **hyperventilation.**

Treat with **ethosuximide or valproate**, and **avoid carbamazepine and phenytoin**, which worsen absence.

The **prognosis is good** — many children outgrow it.
cards.tie: This is the textbook presentation of absence epilepsy and it teaches a genuinely useful clinical lesson — that a child reported as daydreaming or inattentive may be having seizures. The 3 Hz EEG, provocation by hyperventilation, and the warning that carbamazepine and phenytoin worsen absence are all high-yield and directly examinable.

cards.q: KEY CUES: What words, ages and EEG findings should immediately point you to a specific seizure diagnosis or answer?
cards.lecturer: Key cues for seizure disorders: recurrent unprovoked seizures = epilepsy; seizures only with a trigger that stop when removed = provoked. Blank stare, <30 sec, no fall, no postictal, resumes activity, ~5 yr girl, 'daydreaming' = typical absence, EEG 3 Hz spike-and-wave. Infant 4-8 months, brief clusters of flexion/extension spasms, developmental regression = infantile spasms/West syndrome, EEG hypsarrhythmia, treat with ACTH. School-age child, nocturnal seizures, remits in adolescence, centrotemporal spikes on normal background = benign Rolandic epilepsy, drug of choice carbamazepine. Head/eye turning (versive) = lesion in the opposite frontal lobe. Aura = focal onset. Automatisms + impaired awareness + 1-2 min + postictal = temporal lobe (complex partial). Fever 6-60 months, no CNS infection, no prior afebrile seizure = febrile seizure. Simple febrile = <15 min, single, generalized; complex = >15 min, multiple, focal. Seizure >5 min = status epilepticus, first line benzodiazepine. Collapse on exercise/emotion = long QT - do an ECG. Gum hypertrophy = phenytoin. Cushingoid on seizure treatment = ACTH. West syndrome triad = spasms + mental retardation + hypsarrhythmia. Glutamate = excitation, GABA = inhibition. LP especially under 18 months to exclude meningitis. Carbamazepine/phenytoin worsen absence.
cards.explain:
## Group 1 — Definition cues

>> **Recurrent + unprovoked** seizures → **EPILEPSY**

>> **Trigger present, stops when removed** → **PROVOKED** seizure

>> **Normal exam + normal IQ + no lesion** → **genetic (idiopathic)** epilepsy

>> **Structural brain lesion** → **structural (symptomatic)** epilepsy

---

## Group 2 — The four seizure types by signature

>> **Blank stare, < 30 s, no fall, no postictal, ~5 yr girl, "daydreaming"** → **TYPICAL ABSENCE**

>> **Infant 4–8 mo, brief spasm clusters, developmental regression** → **INFANTILE SPASMS / WEST**

>> **School-age, nocturnal, remits in adolescence** → **ROLANDIC epilepsy**

>> **Stiffen then jerk, loss of consciousness** → **GTCS**

---

## Group 3 — EEG cues (guaranteed to appear)

>> **3 Hz spike-and-wave** → **ABSENCE**

>> **Hypsarrhythmia** (chaotic, high-voltage) → **INFANTILE SPASMS / WEST**

>> **Centrotemporal spikes on NORMAL background** → **ROLANDIC**

>> **10–15 Hz spikes then slow waves** → **GTCS**

>> **Focal spikes/sharp waves** → **focal (partial) seizure**

---

## Group 4 — Localising and onset cues

>> **Versive (head/eye turning)** → lesion in the **OPPOSITE frontal lobe**

>> **Aura** → **FOCAL onset**

>> **Automatisms + impaired awareness + 1–2 min + postictal** → **TEMPORAL LOBE** (complex partial)

>> **No aura, generalized from start** → **generalized onset**

---

## Group 5 — Febrile seizure cues

>> **Fever, 6–60 months, no CNS infection, no prior afebrile seizure** → **FEBRILE SEIZURE**

>> **< 15 min, single, generalized** → **SIMPLE**

>> **> 15 min, multiple, focal** → **COMPLEX**

>> **Under 18 months** → **do an LP** (meningitis signs unreliable)

---

## Group 6 — Emergency cues

>> **Seizure > 5 min (or repeated without recovery)** → **STATUS EPILEPTICUS** → first line **benzodiazepine**

>> **Collapse ON EXERCISE / EMOTION** → **LONG QT** → **do an ECG**

>> **GTCS with apnoea/cyanosis** → risk of **hypoxia**

---

## Group 7 — Drug cues

>> **Typical absence** → **ethosuximide** (or valproate)

>> **Infantile spasms** → **ACTH**

>> **Rolandic** → **carbamazepine**

>> **Broad spectrum** → **sodium valproate**

>> **Status, first line** → **benzodiazepine**

>> **GUM HYPERTROPHY** → **phenytoin**

>> **Cushingoid facies on treatment** → **ACTH**

>> **Carbamazepine/phenytoin** → can **WORSEN absence**

---

## Group 8 — Mechanism and rules

>> **Glutamate = excitation (accelerator); GABA = inhibition (brake)**

>> **West syndrome = spasms + mental retardation + hypsarrhythmia**

>> **Epilepsy diagnosis is CLINICAL** — the EEG supports it

>> **Always exclude meningitis before diagnosing febrile seizure**

---

## Putting it all together

Three questions unlock most seizure questions:

**1. Provoked or unprovoked, one-off or recurrent?** → decides whether it is epilepsy.

**2. What is the age + EEG?** → age 5 + 3 Hz = absence; infant + hypsarrhythmia = West; school-age + centrotemporal spikes = Rolandic.

**3. Is there an emergency cue?** → seizure > 5 min = status (benzo first); collapse on exertion = long QT (ECG).

Then reach for the **drug couples** (ethosuximide-absence, ACTH-spasms, carbamazepine-Rolandic) and the **side-effect flags** (phenytoin-gums, ACTH-cushingoid).
cards.tie: These cues are the shortcut between reading a seizure question and knowing the answer. Almost every question in this topic is built around one or two of them — an age plus an EEG pattern, or a trigger plus a drug — and recognising the cue instantly tells you the seizure type, the confirming test, and the correct treatment.
